name: efficientnet

members.name: EfficientNetConfig
members.signature: EfficientNetConfig(num_classes: int = 1000, in_channels: int = 3, width_mult: float = 1.0, depth_mult: float = 1.0, dropout: float = 0.2, drop_connect_rate: float = 0.2, se_ratio: float = 0.25)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(num_classes: int = 1000, in_channels: int = 3, width_mult: float = 1.0, depth_mult: float = 1.0, dropout: float = 0.2, drop_connect_rate: float = 0.2, se_ratio: float = 0.25)

members.extended:
Compound scaling coefficients:
  B0: width=1.0, depth=1.0, res=224, dropout=0.2
  B1: width=1.0, depth=1.1, res=240, dropout=0.2
  B2: width=1.1, depth=1.2, res=260, dropout=0.3
  B3: width=1.2, depth=1.4, res=300, dropout=0.3
  B4: width=1.4, depth=1.8, res=380, dropout=0.4
  B5: width=1.6, depth=2.2, res=456, dropout=0.4
  B6: width=1.8, depth=2.6, res=528, dropout=0.5
  B7: width=2.0, depth=3.1, res=600, dropout=0.5

`drop_connect_rate` — stochastic depth rate applied linearly across blocks.
`se_ratio` — squeeze-and-excitation reduction ratio (0.25 in the paper).

members.name: EfficientNet

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Initialise the ReLU module. See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

parameters.name: features
parameters.description: [P3, P4, P5, P6, P7] (finest → coarsest).

members.extended:
Implements the compound-scaled topology from Tan & Le,
"EfficientNet: Rethinking Model Scaling for Convolutional Neural
Networks", ICML 2019 ([arXiv:1905.11946](https://arxiv.org/abs/1905.11946)).  The baseline (B0) is a
NAS-designed network of MBConv blocks — inverted-residual
bottlenecks (MobileNet-v2 style) augmented with a
squeeze-and-excitation module and the swish (SiLU) activation.
Successive B-variants (B0 through B7) apply the paper's
compound-scaling rule

$$
d = \alpha^\phi, \quad w = \beta^\phi, \quad r = \gamma^\phi,
\qquad \alpha \beta^2 \gamma^2 \approx 2,
$$

to scale depth, width, and resolution jointly along a single
coefficient $\phi$.

The body is a 3×3 stem (stride 2) followed by seven MBConv
stages and a final 1×1 head expansion to `round(1280 ·
width_mult)` channels.  Stochastic depth (`drop_connect_rate`)
is applied linearly across blocks as a strong regulariser at
large depth.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Frozen architecture spec.  Use the factory functions
(`efficientnet_b0` through `efficientnet_b7`)
for paper-cited variants.

examples:
Build an EfficientNet-B0 backbone and run a forward pass:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.efficientnet import efficientnet_b0
>>> backbone = efficientnet_b0()
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = backbone(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape
(2, 1280, 1, 1)
notes:
Each MBConv block applies



$$
x \to \mathrm{Expand}_{1\times1} \to \mathrm{DW}_{k\times k}
    \to \mathrm{SE} \to \mathrm{Proj}_{1\times1},
$$

with a residual when stride is 1 and channel counts match.
During training the residual branch is randomly dropped with
probability `drop_connect_rate * block_idx / (total - 1)` —
deeper blocks get a higher drop probability, matching the
"deep network regularisation" recipe of stochastic depth.

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: features
attributes.description:
Stem + seven MBConv stages + 1×1 head.  Channel and depth
counts scale with `config.width_mult` and
`config.depth_mult`.

attributes.name: avgpool
attributes.description:
Global average pool collapsing the final feature map to
`(B, C, 1, 1)`.

attributes.name: feature_info
attributes.description:
Per-stage descriptor (channels + reduction factor) exposed
via `BackboneMixin` for downstream FPN / decoder
modules.

members.name: EfficientNetForImageClassification

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(x: Tensor, labels: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: EfficientNet with global-average-pooled linear classification head.
members.extended:
Combines an `EfficientNet` backbone with the standard
ImageNet classification head: an `lucid.nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d`
to pool every spatial location into a single feature vector,
a `lucid.nn.Dropout` (probability `config.dropout`,
scaled per B-variant from 0.2 at B0 up to 0.5 at B7), and a
`lucid.nn.Linear` projection to `config.num_classes`
logits.  When `labels` are supplied to `forward`, a
cross-entropy loss is computed and returned alongside the
logits.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Architecture spec.  Use the `*_cls` factory functions
(`efficientnet_b0_cls` through
`efficientnet_b7_cls`) for paper-cited configurations.

examples:
Run inference on a batch of 224×224 RGB images:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.efficientnet import efficientnet_b0_cls
>>> model = efficientnet_b0_cls()
>>> x = lucid.randn(4, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(4, 1000)
Retarget B3 to CIFAR-10:
>>> from lucid.models.vision.efficientnet import efficientnet_b3_cls
>>> model = efficientnet_b3_cls(num_classes=10)
>>> model.config.num_classes
10
notes:
The classification flow is



$$
\text{logits} = W \,\operatorname{Drop}\!\left(
    \operatorname{GAP}(\,\mathrm{backbone}(x)\,)
\right) + b,
$$

with cross-entropy loss computed when `labels` is provided.

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: features
attributes.description: Same MBConv stack as on `EfficientNet`.

attributes.name: avgpool
attributes.description:
Global average pool collapsing the final feature map to
`1 × 1`.

attributes.name: drop
attributes.description:
Dropout layer (probability `config.dropout`) applied
before the linear classifier.

attributes.name: classifier
attributes.description:
Linear projection from `round(1280 · width_mult)` to
`config.num_classes`.

members.name: efficientnet_b0
members.extended:
Builds an `EfficientNet` with the NAS-designed baseline
topology from Tan & Le, 2019 (Table 1): seven MBConv stages
with block repeats `(1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4, 1)`, channel
progression `16 → 24 → 40 → 80 → 112 → 192 → 320`, and a
1×1 head expansion to 1280 channels.  Approximately 5.3M
parameters and 77.1% ImageNet-1k top-1 accuracy at the
224×224 native resolution (Table 2).

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently
ignored — the returned model is randomly initialised.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides forwarded into `EfficientNetConfig`.

returns.description:
Backbone with the B0 configuration applied (or with
`overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.efficientnet import efficientnet_b0
>>> model = efficientnet_b0()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape
(1, 1280, 1, 1)
notes:
See Tan & Le, "EfficientNet: Rethinking Model Scaling for
Convolutional Neural Networks", ICML 2019 ([arXiv:1905.11946](https://arxiv.org/abs/1905.11946)),
Table 1 and Table 2.

members.name: efficientnet_b0_cls
members.signature: efficientnet_b0_cls(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: EfficientNetB0Weights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.summary: EfficientNet-B0 image classifier (backbone + GAP + linear head).
members.extended:
Builds an `EfficientNetForImageClassification` with the
NAS-designed B0 topology followed by global average pooling,
dropout ($p = 0.2$), and a linear projection to
`config.num_classes` (default 1000 for ImageNet-1k).
Approximately 5.3M parameters and 77.1% ImageNet-1k top-1
accuracy at 224×224 (Tan & Le, 2019, Table 2).

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Pretrained-weight selector.  `False` → random init; `True`
→ the `DEFAULT` tag
(`EfficientNetB0Weights.IMAGENET1K_V1`); a tag string
(e.g. `"IMAGENET1K_V1"`) → that specific checkpoint.
Mutually exclusive with `weights` (which wins if both given).

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description:
Explicit weights enum member, e.g.
`EfficientNetB0Weights.IMAGENET1K_V1`.  Takes precedence over
`pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides forwarded into `EfficientNetConfig`
(typically `num_classes` to retarget the classifier).
Overriding `num_classes` away from the checkpoint's class
count makes pretrained loading fail the strict key/shape check.

returns.description:
Classifier with the B0 configuration applied (or with
`overrides` merged on top of it), optionally initialised from
pretrained weights.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.efficientnet import efficientnet_b0_cls
>>> model = efficientnet_b0_cls(num_classes=10)
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(2, 10)
Load ImageNet-pretrained weights:
>>> model = efficientnet_b0_cls(pretrained=True)            # DEFAULT tag
notes:
See Tan & Le, "EfficientNet: Rethinking Model Scaling for
Convolutional Neural Networks", ICML 2019 ([arXiv:1905.11946](https://arxiv.org/abs/1905.11946)),
Table 2.  Pretrained weights are converted from the
reference-framework `EfficientNet_B0_Weights` and hosted on the
Hugging Face Hub under `lucid-dl/efficientnet-b0`.

members.name: efficientnet_b1
members.extended:
Builds an `EfficientNet` with depth multiplier 1.1 and
width multiplier 1.0 — same channel counts as B0 but with
block repeats scaled up by `ceil(n · 1.1)`.  Approximately
7.8M parameters and 79.1% ImageNet-1k top-1 accuracy at the
240×240 native resolution (Tan & Le, 2019, Table 2).

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently
ignored.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides forwarded into `EfficientNetConfig`.

returns.description:
Backbone with the B1 configuration applied (or with
`overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.efficientnet import efficientnet_b1
>>> model = efficientnet_b1()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 240, 240)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape
(1, 1280, 1, 1)
notes:
See Tan & Le, "EfficientNet: Rethinking Model Scaling for
Convolutional Neural Networks", ICML 2019 ([arXiv:1905.11946](https://arxiv.org/abs/1905.11946)),
Table 2.

members.summary: EfficientNet-B1 image classifier (backbone + GAP + linear head).
members.extended:
Builds an `EfficientNetForImageClassification` with the
B1 compound-scaling configuration (depth × 1.1, width × 1.0)
followed by dropout ($p = 0.2$) and a linear classifier.
Approximately 7.8M parameters and 79.1% ImageNet-1k top-1
accuracy at 240×240 (Tan & Le, 2019, Table 2).

notes:

reference-framework `EfficientNet_B1_Weights` and hosted under

followed by dropout ($p = 0.5$) and a linear classifier.
Approximately 43M parameters and 84.0% ImageNet-1k top-1
accuracy at 528×528 (Tan & Le, 2019, Table 2).

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Pretrained-weight selector.  `False` → random init; `True`
→ the `DEFAULT` tag
(`EfficientNetB6Weights.IMAGENET1K_V1`); a tag string →
that specific checkpoint.  Mutually exclusive with `weights`.

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description:
Explicit weights enum member.  Takes precedence over
`pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides forwarded into `EfficientNetConfig`.

returns.description:
Classifier with the B6 configuration applied (or with
`overrides` merged on top of it), optionally initialised from
pretrained weights.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.efficientnet import efficientnet_b6_cls
>>> model = efficientnet_b6_cls(pretrained=True)
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 528, 528)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(1, 1000)
notes:
See Tan & Le, "EfficientNet: Rethinking Model Scaling for
Convolutional Neural Networks", ICML 2019 ([arXiv:1905.11946](https://arxiv.org/abs/1905.11946)),
Table 2.  Pretrained weights are converted from the
reference-framework `EfficientNet_B6_Weights` and hosted under
`lucid-dl/efficientnet-b6`.

members.name: efficientnet_b7
members.extended:
Builds an `EfficientNet` with width multiplier 2.0 and
depth multiplier 3.1 — the largest variant in the original
paper.  Approximately 66M parameters and 84.4% ImageNet-1k
top-1 accuracy at the 600×600 native resolution (Tan & Le,
2019, Table 2), state-of-the-art at the time of publication.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently
ignored.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides forwarded into `EfficientNetConfig`.

returns.description:
Backbone with the B7 configuration applied (or with
`overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.efficientnet import efficientnet_b7
>>> model = efficientnet_b7()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 600, 600)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape
(1, 2560, 1, 1)
notes:
See Tan & Le, "EfficientNet: Rethinking Model Scaling for
Convolutional Neural Networks", ICML 2019 ([arXiv:1905.11946](https://arxiv.org/abs/1905.11946)),
Table 2.  Head expansion: `round(1280 · 2.0) = 2560`
channels.  Memory footprint at 600×600 is substantial — plan
accordingly for training.

members.name: efficientnet_b7_cls
members.signature: efficientnet_b7_cls(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: EfficientNetB7Weights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.summary: EfficientNet-B7 image classifier (backbone + GAP + linear head).
members.extended:
Builds an `EfficientNetForImageClassification` with the
B7 compound-scaling configuration (width × 2.0, depth × 3.1)
followed by dropout ($p = 0.5$) and a linear classifier —
the largest variant in the original paper.  Approximately
66M parameters and 84.4% ImageNet-1k top-1 accuracy at
600×600 (Tan & Le, 2019, Table 2), state-of-the-art at the
time of publication.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Pretrained-weight selector.  `False` → random init; `True`
→ the `DEFAULT` tag
(`EfficientNetB7Weights.IMAGENET1K_V1`); a tag string →
that specific checkpoint.  Mutually exclusive with `weights`.

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description:
Explicit weights enum member.  Takes precedence over
`pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides forwarded into `EfficientNetConfig`.

returns.description:
Classifier with the B7 configuration applied (or with
`overrides` merged on top of it), optionally initialised from
pretrained weights.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.efficientnet import efficientnet_b7_cls
>>> model = efficientnet_b7_cls(pretrained=True)
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 600, 600)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(1, 1000)
notes:
See Tan & Le, "EfficientNet: Rethinking Model Scaling for
Convolutional Neural Networks", ICML 2019 ([arXiv:1905.11946](https://arxiv.org/abs/1905.11946)),
Table 2.  At 600×600 input resolution memory usage is
substantial — plan inference accordingly.  Pretrained weights are
converted from the reference-framework `EfficientNet_B7_Weights`
and hosted under `lucid-dl/efficientnet-b7`.

attributes.name: DEFAULT
attributes.description: Alias for `IMAGENET1K_V1`.

citation: Tan, Mingxing, and Quoc V. Le. "EfficientNet: Rethinking Model Scaling for Convolutional Neural Networks." Proceedings of the 36th International Conference on Machine Learning, PMLR 97, 2019, pp. 6105–6114.
theory:
EfficientNet is built on the empirical observation that the three
axes used to scale a ConvNet — **depth** $d$ (number of
layers), **width** $w$ (number of channels per layer), and
input **resolution** $r$ — are not independent.  Scaling any
one of them in isolation gives rapidly diminishing returns, but
scaling all three together along a carefully chosen ratio gives a
much better accuracy/FLOPs frontier.

The paper formalises this as **compound scaling**: a single
user-facing scalar $\phi$ controls all three axes through



$$
d = \alpha^\phi, \quad
w = \beta^\phi, \quad
r = \gamma^\phi,
$$

subject to the FLOPs-balance constraint
$\alpha \cdot \beta^2 \cdot \gamma^2 \approx 2$.  The
constants $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are found once by a
small grid search on the baseline network (giving roughly
$\alpha = 1.2, \beta = 1.1, \gamma = 1.15$); from then on,
a single $\phi$ indexes the whole family.  Successive
integer values produce the standard B0 → B7 variants, each
requiring approximately $2^\phi$ more FLOPs than B0.

The baseline B0 itself is *not* manually designed.  It is the
output of a multi-objective neural-architecture search that
optimises accuracy *and* FLOPs jointly.  Each block is an
inverted-residual MBConv (MobileNet-v2 style) augmented with a
squeeze-and-excitation module, and the network uses the swish
activation throughout.  **Stochastic depth** with linearly
increasing drop rate (the `drop_connect_rate` field) acts as
a strong regulariser as depth grows, which is what allows
B6/B7 to keep gaining accuracy where naively-deepened
baselines would overfit or fail to train.
